transcript.transcript: My name is Estella Burque. I'll be the interviewer for today. I'm a Diversity and Inclusion Summer Apprentice at the LA Public Library. Today is Friday, August 15th, and we are recording at Bob Baker Marionette Theater in Highland Park. Can you please introduce yourself and say how you would like to be identified, and what exactly is your role here at Bob Baker? Yeah, my name is Alex Evans, he/him pronouns. I am the co-executive director and artistic director of the Bob Baker Marionette Theater. Perfect. Okay, so, I mean, I'm very eager to just get right into it. So we want to start off with a little bit about your childhood and your early years. So we are, you know, wondering where you were born, where did you grow up and, yeah. Yeah, yeah. So I was born in Jersey, which is like a British Channel Island on the—It's close. It's closer to France than England, but owned by England. The biggest thing in my mind about Jersey is that it was the Nazis.—It was a English territory that the Nazis occupied—occupied in the war. But other than that, it's a tiny little island. But I was two when I moved—I think it was also famous for being a tax haven for, like, wealthy people. Oh, yeah. But not we were there. But then I moved when I was two, I moved to England proper, which is truly like, if I did DNAandMe [reference to 23andMe], 100% English for generations, and that is where I grew up until I was ten years old, moved to New England until I was 15, then went to Georgia, then went to school in New York, and then moved to California. Yep. And here we are. Yeah. So moving around that much, I guess—How did that shape the experience of growing up? I—I moved around a lot too, but all in Los—I was just moving to different places in Los Angeles. So it's very different than crossing the ocean. I feel like it's probably not that much different, because it is—It's just a kind of change of norms. It was definitely when—England to America, it was definitely the biggest cultural move. But it's not as if England and America have giant different cultures. It's like—so—but that was—at the end of the day, I was still this eleven-year-old kid. It was—you know, going to school and things like that. The—so—but I mean, that said, it is—I—I often think, or, I've kind of visited kind of like—my childhood friends from England who had lived in the same place, never moved, and they've lived in the same town. And now are the local electricians or families that move in the house and they're there and they have this deep, deep, deep, deep, deep connection. Or actually, I shouldn't even say that, I don't know. But in my mind they do. And I think I've always been envious, may be the wrong word, but I think it's like—oh, there's a consistency there that I never had, at the same time, I could imagine they would probably say—oh—I wish—they had more varied experiences. This was just a recognition of—difference, I guess. Really? Yeah, definitely. Okay, and then so—kind of to go off of that a little bit in, in thinking about the communities that you grew up in, I'm really interested to know what role—did creativity play in—or if creativity played any role—but if creativity played a role in your childhood and kind of—who were, you know, some of your influencers and supporters in that? I mean, I think my mother's the one that comes that comes to mind. And I mean, in a very—my mom was like a home economics teacher, and it was very much in—you know, making birthday cakes in creative ways. I wouldn't—I mean, she would never call herself— her, even—even an artist or anything like that. I think it was just—she kind of had creativity ingrained in kind of the little things that she did that were all very homespun of like—hey—this is the way to make a scrapbook or things like that. I think—so that I got a lot out of. It's just very kind of—just creative in everyday life. She—Beavers, which is the English version of, what's before Boy Scouts? There's something else. Cub Scouts? Yeah. Like she—would do the Halloween things with that and kind of tell stories of—like, put your hands in this bag and it's eyeballs, but it's grapes, and things like that. But like that—I—at the very least, it still sticks in my mind. And then, I mean—other kind of—creative inspirations, were, I mean, like, I—I definitely did a lot of art in school and was, you know, that level of something where it's like—hey—I'm kind of good at this. I'm not—great at this or even not that driven to kind of like—hey—I'm gonna go and do oil painting or even—or in sports. They're probably like—oh, I'm not gonna be the captain of the football team, but they know how to kick a ball. So it's like—it always kind of was at that level, which meant that I kind of didn't—I felt very, maybe unfocused, is the word, but it was like—oh—I love riding a bike. I love painting, I love making something out of cardboard. I love—I just love it all. So, that—yeah, I kind of—I—I can't—I can't think of one specific influence besides my mother and—but if anything, my memories of it is like—oh—very eclectic. Yeah, definitely. So then what was elementary school and middle school and high school, too, what was that like for you? In general, honestly. I mean, so this—so sixth grade is America, and that is—I mean—that's kind of—you know—adolescence—like, who am I? And very—new place, very, very shy. To this day at the puppet theater when my mother—kind of—comes, she's shocked that I'm in front of people talking because—it was just—my—especially in high school, it was like—oh—I'm gonna hide. I'm just really just—don't want to be there or like the—like the English role was a thing, and they kind of— I have an older brother, they even—not in a bullying way—but would call him Pip, which sounds like Great Expectations. It's South Park is what I would go with that. Yeah. And they—it was just a very clear role to play that was both good or bad. Everyone's kind of looking for identity, kind of, at that point in their life. So it was this pretty, kind of easy one to fall into Although very aware at the moment, a very superficial one. Yes, yeah. So that was—when I was like 15, I moved to Georgia and that was two years before graduating high school and naturally just felt like a countdown. It was just like—oh, no, like—go on with the rest of my life after I'm through with this and that—I doubt anybody in high school knows my name or remembers me at all. Yeah. And—on purpose would be to give myself too credit. It's like—I would be just a shy, awkward kid. Yeah. And then, but just was like—okay, I'm gonna be gone in two years, so— Yeah —I was gone. Yeah, so I think—well, what's really interesting, then, I think, is that, you know—soon after you—you graduated high school, you did move here to LA. Mhm. And like pretty—I mean kind of broke out—I think, or my interpretation is kind of breaking out of that role of, of shyness especially, and kind of getting involved with the theater. And I'm wondering if anything specific brought you to Los Angeles or was it—I mean—I know for me, even growing up here, I still have the idea of LA that I'm—I—I'm still looking for. Yeah. Yeah. I think that is—I mean—the thing that brought me here was the movies. I was—I went to school for film photography. It's like—oh, LA is where I'm going to get an internship and be writing movies. And also LA is like the American Dream at least. Or, you know, it's sunshine and lights and it's great. And so that's like why I was here to kind of get an internship on a film thing. But—and so—kind of looking to explore, but looking for—Because when I found the theater, the theater was not what the program was designed for. It was—I googled, looking for stop motion or kind of animation things and then discovered this strange building under a bridge that was very sketchy on the outside and magical on the inside. And, and that really was my gateway drug to LA, which I think is like—it is LA is this glitz and glamour of the movies and the romantic ideas—like—I'm here and I love that idealized romanticism to the city. But then it also has these deep, deep, deep layers of fascinating reality, history. So, both things are true at once. And, and the theater was like such a good example of that, I think especially the old theater where—I say old theater, meaning the 1345 that—that was—it's a place where you like—oh, you kind of think this is this upper class puppet theater, and it's kind of good. But like, the staff were kids from the neighborhood, people I would never—I think, meet kind of continuing in life, working on a set or whatever, You've seen—you know Cheers? The TV show Cheers,—like the best TV show of all time.—That I think that—like—that's the—What I love about Cheers is it's kind of a show about street smarts and book smarts. Yeah. And the puppet theater, it was like—people were just from a different walk of life than I was, and like—truly, that's like, kind of from a very classist point of view. Like middle class upbringing, meeting kind of the very, very working class kind of neighborhood of Echo Park. Yeah. And I—they—like I am empirically just more educated than them. And they are—I shouldn't say that—but essentially I met people that were just smarter than me. Like what—not having that experience. And it was just their mind opening way of seeing the world and seeing people. Yeah. And in the theater—just for all the obvious reasons of how it was magic, there was like that layer of just magical interpersonal community. Yeah. Yeah. So I feel like once that door was open, like, I was like, I was I was hooked. Yeah. And was that—Was that your—first experience with puppetry at all? Was visiting Bob Baker? Yeah. In any kind of formal sense, I guess. So stop motion. It was like from high school. I used to make stop motion movies and things, but even—even saying that, I feel like—is giving myself too much credit. Yeah. And so I was interested in stop motion puppets, but, not marionettes. I remember when I was a kid, my dad gave me this Thunderbird toy set, which was like an old English marionette TV show. And I remember kind of—just kind of being a brat about it being like—oh, like puppets—like, this is kind of silly like, so it truly was just naive, and then when I saw it at the theater, it—the experience was mind blowing. Similar to the experiences seeing Freaky Friday—is that it was like—oh, I didn't expect this to be this captivating, this magical. And yeah, that—that's it. And then from then, I feel like I fell in love with the theater first and then through that, a deep appreciation and love of puppetry now, but truly, through the lens of the theater. Yeah, absolutely. So then, I mean, it is—it is reasonable to assume, you know—this isn't necessarily what you—you know—came to LA for and kind of that kind of thing. And so I'm wondering kind of what the—I don't know—what I guess the emotional or interpersonal act of—you know, you came here intentionally for film and then to kind of pivot—so like almost 180 to public theater or puppetry, and kind of what that was like. Yeah. I mean, it never felt like a pivot or anything like that—in that it always felt different than what everyone else in my class was doing. But like, not—the theater has so much connection to Hollywood and history like I used to go to the Academy screenings with Bob and like, was—had access to, you know, as much Hollywood as anyone else in the film program. The—but—yeah, I mean, I definitely was aware that this was different, but it was like—I didn't approach the theater being like, hey, this is my forever thing. Yeah Yeah, up until 2015 maybe, I was still, like—used to doing editing stuff. I used to stay up all night, edit it, and kind of—go work at the theater in the days. So I was still pursuing or thinking of that as the livelihood option until—and at the time of the theater, the idea of just getting a paycheck period was something that was almost a silly question because the theater just was in such need. Yeah. So I think I was never confronted with that question. I was like—oh, this is different than what we planned. Yeah, definitely. I'm also really curious to know—Well, I guess this question could be asked in two ways. Like—what it was like for you to start working here and to kind of just—start your journey. But also because I am wondering—is—are there puppetry classes and kind of like—a track that you follow in order to work in this type of art form or theater. So there definitely can be and there are, and not for—not for me. I mean, not at the theater. Like I said, when I started the theater, I found the theater through a Google search, I called up and had to convince them to let me come down. They did, saw the show, fell in love, and then convinced Bob—being like—hey, this will be—you know, I want to be here. And he was like—all right. You're, you know, a film intern, like, really go do other places. Like, I had to talk myself into it. And then when I was there, it was a little bit like open season, like—no internship program that was telling you, like—hey, you're gonna do this and this, and here's your supervisor or whatever it is. It was truly kind of whatever. Yeah. And then the puppeteers at the time, like Scotty Castillo and Cain Carias, who—Cain is @puppetmaster213, who's now El Triste, the saddest clown in Los Angeles. But they were very just, like, kind and sweet and very much welcomed me in. And part of it was the culture of the theater at the time was very—it was like—they didn't want to do the heavy puppets like—hey, here's this young kid that would pick up the heavy puppets. And they loved it. They loved the theater, loved it. But they like—you know, they weren't kind of from the standpoint of apprenticeship or classically trained kind of performers. It was not that, it was like—hey, yeah, pick up this thing, do this. Like, I'm gonna go take a break. Yeah. And so that, and then and then at the same time, that's in stark contrast with Bob who was there, and Bob is trained through and through. Bob grew up, started performing when he was five, movement and tap, and was like—you know, truly Baby Jane. Yeah. Before that and—trained with this golden age of marionettes in LA and did all this incredible stuff. So there was—there's this—there was that ingrained history, or, kind of sense around, and then always a sense of like—oh, Bob knows how this is done or what's the right way to do it. It wasn't as if this way that I came in just up the street was the like—oh, that was like the way then, it was just like, that's big. That's—it was just the time. Yeah. Yeah. So, like it kind of—we—we do auditions at the theater. When we hire new puppeteers we have these days. Like two hundred people apply and we kind of bring them in and they—it's like this kind of puppet karaoke. They kind of—we give them twenty minutes where they kind of pick up a puppet, and everyone has a song that they've selected, the idea being that they know the song. Yeah. And then, you know, there's no expectation for them to master this thing, but like, what are they going to pick up in twenty minutes? And how are they going to express themselves like on stage? And then I would like—it would have taken me years to pass that test. I never would have been able to kind of become a puppeteer. Yeah. And so it—I would say I was like—probably three years into working at the theater before I was scratching the surface of feeling like a good performer or that I could have conversations with other puppeteers of like—hey, we're on the level of like—oh, you do, you know, this, control this thing or what strings do you use? And they go just—you know, the whole—all the silly kind of talk. And part of that was like, I remember the LA Guild of Puppetry, whose—long legacy, wonderful organization—at the time, they were like a bunch of old grumpy people. I mean that very respectfully, but—you know, they saw Bob Baker at the time as the scruffy kids and they—you know, kind of just looked down at us and in a way that was honestly fair, right? We were clearly an organization that kind of had its heyday—like, wasn't in it at the moment. And, you know, they had studied puppetry. Yeah. And we were just—happened to be there really. And so there was almost a validation of kind of being independent, or, kind of just doing. And by independent, like we were a tight community of people bonding over loving the theater. But, you know, it's it felt very much like we're carving our own way. Yeah. Which I as I recount this, it's like—it is like there was always that kind of Obi-Wan that was Bob Baker just floating around. Yeah. But— I trying to figure out how many movie references I can get into it. Yeah. So how—I mean, I have been coming to Bob Baker since I was a kid, and I've been to countless shows. And so, how many do you—or, do you even have an estimate on how many puppets you guys work with or have in the collection? Yeah, it's a really hard question to answer. It's like we say three thousand. It's like a relatively true, easy, big, impressive number. But like there's probably—we've recently did the great puppet photo shoot where we took a photo of every puppet. Oh, wow. And that, I want to say, that was like fifteen hundred. Maybe a little less than that, actually maybe it was close to a thousand. I can't remember, but it kind of—a little bit that's like—hey, here's this, beautiful, fully functioning object. And then even within that, this kind of ambiguous things of like—here's a puppet that is three characters controlled by one control. Yeah. Yeah. And is that one puppet or three in a row? So there's—so yeah, but that's like counting the stage ones we perform. Yes. Yeah. But we also, you know, build puppets that are kind of sold. Or there's toy lines that we have that are beautiful handmade objects. But you count that towards the collection too. So that's—that's where the 3000 comes from. So— so we have a lot of puppets. Yeah. Yeah. And I, I have frequently noticed, a lot of the same puppets were reappearing in so many different shows and, some of my favorite puppets of Bob Baker's—thinking about it, was I—my parents would take me to the Halloween show every single year. Yeah. And so I loved the three trick-or-treating kids. Yeah. And then I was on the Bob Baker website, and I realized—or, I saw a photo that, you know, those three trick-or-treating kids are puppets that are put into Halloween costumes and used. Roddy, Clara and Fred from The Nutcracker. Yes, exactly. Yeah. And because then I realized that I think one of the reasons why they stuck out to me so much is because I had also been to The Nutcracker shows. And I wonder if, in my mind, I had, kind of recognized them and pulled them together. Like placing Philip Seymour Hoffman in a movie. Yes, exactly. And so what is it like? You know, with puppets that are are being used in so many of the same shows, but also, you guys build puppets here and have a lot of very, very old puppets. I'm interested in the mechanics of either, building a new puppet, repairing an older puppet, or deciding when— like if puppets retire and if there's kind of a sign that, like—it's time for this puppet to go. Yeah. Great question. Like—and there's like many different answers to that. So Halloween's actually a strangely new show. So Bob had Dracula, Vampira and the witches, they're all from the 1959 Get Happy, which is just a variety show. But like, at the time, Halloween was like two weekends, right, and it never really had a long enough run to justify mounting a show. Yes. Yeah. Until like probably 2025—or 2005, like—2005. That was the first time we did Halloween for the whole month. Now it's our most popular show. Yeah. This year's opening in September. And—Halloween, when I started Halloween, It was like—It truly was just like—very clearly just hodgepodged together quickly, kind of like, added. And so that was just us experimenting. Yeah. And it was really interesting period, because Bob was 80—late 80s at that point, and you can look at some of the Halloween puppets of that period, it was clearly just toys, kind—of converted into things. And Clara, Fred and Roddy, who were the characters that became trick-or-treaters, that was just—that was—there was this—Bob, I think had passed away, and we went through this phase of trying to kind of restore, make everything the most it can be, and we just didn't have the capacity to make a puppet, which was hundreds of hours, like a lot of money. So we were like—okay, we can—the girl—or, the wife of a puppeteer does costumes. Here's the—here's this Disney trick or treat reference, the King's family variety show is where the music's from. So—and then like—that was very specifically supposed to be Clara from Nutcracker in her Halloween costume. Oh, wow. Yeah. So it like—it means a lot that that that clocked for you. Yeah. Because, like, it is—we have this weird, I say weird because it's things that feel a certain way, only to us, those who are close to them. Where it's like—oh, the black cat only sings this type of song, this is her personality in this costume. Where, like—very fairly, anyone else from the outside would be like—ah you know, make it do this or do whatever it is. And, you know, we have a clear idea in our heads what it is. But there's—it's very much you pick and choose the line because like—it's called the black cat. It's like. No, like, that is like—their personalities only been developed so far. Yeah, yeah. And then honestly it's only been developed as far as this three minute number on stage. Yeah. And the—so like—yeah. It's the idea of using puppets for other things is always—it just hits emotionally, kind of. It's like, ah,—it doesn't feel right. And yeah, to us it's like Mickey Mouse or Kermit, It's like, ah—that's who they are. Yeah. You know, you don't want Kermit doing anything other than being a frog. Yeah, yeah. So we try and keep to that. At least, at least that logic line. Like, to me right now, I think it's just some type of hint around it all is the kind of everything, even if it doesn't all add up, essentially kind of a way that you can kind of spin a story that will—it doesn't even need to make sense. It just needs to be fun or silly. So Clara having a Halloween costume is silly and fun. The the really interesting question that we deal with—with the fixing puppets is that like—So the oldest—we have Bob's very first puppet here, which is this little wooden soldier, it's not even a wooden soldier, it's a little wooden sailor. Probably from 1931, and that's the oldest puppet that we have. We have puppets from the '40s that are still actively used. There's like—the first puppets that ever performed at Disneyland are still used on our stage, these historical objects are there. And, you know, all of them to some degree are wearing. Fabric tears. Yeah. And you think like—and it's you know,—there definitely was a long period, and it still is—like, hey is this thing too beat up to just use and just—looks bad and no longer delivers on the intention of what it's supposed to be? And then when you go to fix it, they don't make this fabric anymore. They don't—it's like, do you replace it or do you retire it? And that's where we've with—I don't think we've ever like really actively retired something, but Bob's first puppet sailor, like that's that's an historical like. Yes. Yeah definitely But like Feathers, who was this 1948 clown that was in the window, like that's made to perform. It's made to kind of do that. There's a magic and a charm that this same puppet performed, you know, to—four generations of families. And if it's not performing—you know, you would be stripping it of a certain life, that bring it in the case. So that's where we land. And then—but then there's just a lot of pretty difficult creative decisions about like—how far do you go? Like, even if you can make improvements, you can. But that is true of the whole history of theater. Like there is—if you like—look at any puppet you can see that you're in conversation with all the different craftsmen. There's little signatures, but unintentional signatures, of different improvements or changes that they've made over the years. So it feels true to the legacy to keep, you know, restoring and updating and changing, I think is like—When we say living theater a lot, this is what we mean by that, it's not supposed to be—When I started, there was like a patina of dust everywhere, and it hadn't been touched for a while. But that was only in this late eighties, nineties period. Before that, the theater was, every season, doing this beautiful refresh and changing. And like that is like—that's who the theater is, this kind of living, changing thing. Yeah. Do you—I know we have—I mean, we've talked a bit about favorites and that kind of thing, but do you have a favorite puppet? So, so this is a great question that hits at the heart of who I really am as a person. LikeDemetrius Nova Twinklestar the ThirdI have like go-to favorites that I say to—because the question comes up all the time, like, so, Demetrius Nova Twinklestar the Third, the tap dancing frogs, Jack Frost, they're just fun ones to perform. I love those designs. It's great. They're good. But like, honestly, it's like—that's only part—that's really kind of just from the notion of it's—oh, it's good to have favorites, it's good to have things. It's fun. It's like not a really satisfying answer to say like—oh, they're all like the favorites. But it's pretty true that they're each so special in their own way. And you can tell with performers that like there's like a learning curve where it is the simplest puppets that can do the most beautiful things that you would, you know, you look past. It'd be like, just taking it on it's like—oh, how elaborate is this dance? Or what does it look like. Some of them are just balls of yarn that shimmy and then. You can make that kind of sell. Then it's really just as powerful as anything else. Yeah, definitely. I mean, I think—I remember seeing Bob Baker shows and after every number, saying to myself, like—that's the coolest puppet. Like, that's my favorite. And then it would be the next one. I'd be like—oh my God, I have to choose again. And so I definitely—I definitely really get that. It's really interesting. Would you agree that that's part of the charm? That the quantity or the amount—like it feels to me it feels never ending? Yes, yeah. It's like really—like, oh, wow. There's this whole world of imagination that could be behind that curtain. Yeah. And my, I mean, my favorite—one of my favorite things throughout life has been bringing people, or being around people who are at Bob Baker for their first time. Especially for shows that I have very distinct memories of. Where, like when I go to the Halloween show, I used to go every year. And when I would go—I sitting in the theater, I could say to myself, like, this is what's next. And then it's this and this and this. But I always loved, especially, I think, watching young kids see that for the first time in the creativity of of so much. And I'm thinking about the flying bed puppet, and so many things where it's like—when it starts, you have no idea where it's gonna end. And I think that that is something that I've always really loved about Bob Baker, is that like—and even being someone that has seen the same shows a lot, it always—like it never feels like boring. It always almost feels like the first time, where it's like you get to see it all again. Yeah. That's great. It is like—we talk a lot in the theater about the shared experience or like—that is like—To this day, there's just as many people when we ask at the top of the show, like—how people were here for the first time, it's like—it's always been half the audience. And I think there's—I guess I think the—what you described, I think is a very shared thing of, people enjoy sharing it with each other. And I think that is something that has been paramount to us, to the to the magic. It's not—it's just as much about your individual experience as it is about like—oh, I can share this with someone or the audience right there. Yeah. And I think like, what also for me is always really fun, is that it truly is for all ages. Whereas I feel like—if you were to say the phrase like puppet theater to someone, I'm sure their assumption would be like—it's for children. But, you know, I remember getting the free ice cream after the shows growing up and then I came for the New Year's show last year, and then because it was all adults, instead of ice cream, it was the champagne instead. Yeah. And so, like—I think, you know—I know that the LA Public Library definitely prides itself in kind of still existing as one of these third places. And I think Bob Baker works in a very similar way of it not just being for kids or not just being for adults, but a place where anyone can come and enjoy it. I totally agree. And I do think there's this muddiness that happens with puppets and kids and things like that where like—I think you just described it so well, where it's not about, you know, children's entertainment. It's about like—oh, feeling safe like a kid does, or like I think that is really, like totally what happens here. Because honestly, Bob used to hate the idea of kid's show or adult show they're all just shows. Definitely. But I do think there is a power and a realism to it being—oh, it is kids all ages. But that just that just means like, oh, you just feel sick. All the pros of being a kid that adults want to feel again. Yeah. That is—Yeah, it's totally true. Definitely, and I think, you know, while we're a little bit on the topic of specifics and that kind of thing—also, you know, because Bob Baker runs a lot of the same shows for holidays and that kind of thing, but also there are a lot of new shows that that pop up. And so I'm really interested in what the—I guess—I mean for new shows, what the development process is of creating and choreographing a new show, but then also for all shows in general, rehearsing and practicing and how that works here. Yeah. So we haven't done a new show in 40 years. So like our first ground up, 100 new puppets, new show, we're in the middle of making it right now. It opens in June and so that's been a process like—and mainly a little bit of us like figuring out the answers to the questions—but essentially we do a lot of—essentially what happened was that Bob made—Bob did all these shows at Laguna Arts Festival. In 1958, 59, 60 and 61. And then that was what is the The Crow About, The Circus, Get Happy—which should just become a variety show—and Sketchbook Review—which is just a variety show, but like, that's the baseline of what is Bob Baker. And then Bob opened the theater in 1963. And the theater really—I think Bob bought the theater being like—oh, this is going to be great and easy. I'm gonna have a theater. I can house, all these things. I make new shows. I think the undertaking of operating a theater was more than Bob anticipated. And people used to joke that Bob only had a theater to support his—support his puppet making habit. lines.line: # Childhood and Early Years

> Alex Evans shares details about his birthplace in Jersey, a British Channel Island, and his family's moves to England, New England, Georgia, and eventually California. He reflects on the impact of moving frequently, comparing his experience to friends who stayed in one place, and discusses the sense of consistency and varied experiences that shaped his upbringing.

> Alex credits his mother as his primary creative influence, describing her home economics background and creative activities like making birthday cakes and scrapbooks. The theatre's resilience and the community's support were crucial in navigating the challenges of the pandemic.

children.text: # Introduction and Interview Setup

> The interviewer, Estella Burke, introduces herself, the date, and the location of the interview at Bob Baker Marionette Theatre. She asks Alex Evans to introduce himself, specify his pronouns, and describe his role at the theatre. This opening establishes the context and participants for the conversation.
# Childhood and Early Years

> Alex Evans shares details about his birthplace in Jersey, a British Channel Island, and his family's moves to England, New England, Georgia, and eventually California. He reflects on the impact of moving frequently, comparing his experience to friends who stayed in one place, and discusses the sense of consistency and varied experiences that shaped his upbringing.
# Creativity in Childhood and Influences

> Alex credits his mother as his primary creative influence, describing her home economics background and creative activities like making birthday cakes and scrapbooks. He recalls her involvement in youth groups and storytelling, and notes his own eclectic interests in art, biking, and crafts, emphasizing the everyday creativity that surrounded him growing up.
# School Experiences and Adolescence

> Alex discusses his transition to American schools in sixth grade, his shyness, and the search for identity during adolescence. He describes feeling out of place, the role of being 'the English kid,' and his tendency to keep a low profile, especially after moving to Georgia for high school. He reflects on the countdown to graduation and his lack of lasting connections during this period.
# Moving to Los Angeles and First Encounter with Bob Baker Theatre

> Alex explains that his move to Los Angeles was motivated by his interest in film and photography, seeking internships in the movie industry. He describes discovering the Bob Baker Marionette Theatre through a Google search, being captivated by its magical atmosphere, and how this unexpected encounter became his gateway to LA's deeper cultural layers and community.
# Introduction to Puppetry and Early Experiences at Bob Baker

> Alex recounts his early interest in stop motion animation and his initial skepticism toward marionettes. He describes his first formal experience with puppetry at Bob Baker, where he was amazed by the magic and artistry of the performances. His appreciation for puppetry grew through his involvement with the theatre, which became central to his creative life.
# Transition from Film to Puppetry and Emotional Impact

> Alex discusses how working at Bob Baker felt distinct from his film studies, yet remained connected to Hollywood and history. He describes balancing editing work with theatre duties, the financial challenges of the theatre, and the gradual realization that his path was diverging from his original film ambitions. The emotional and interpersonal aspects of this shift are highlighted.
# Learning Puppetry and Culture at Bob Baker

> Alex explains that while formal puppetry classes exist, his own training at Bob Baker was informal and hands-on. He describes the welcoming culture among puppeteers, the apprenticeship-like learning process, and the contrast between Bob Baker's traditional expertise and the scruffy, independent spirit of the staff. He reflects on the validation and community that shaped his development as a puppeteer.
# The Puppet Collection: Numbers, Reuse, and Restoration

> Alex estimates the theatre's puppet collection at around 3,000, though recent photo documentation suggests a lower number. He discusses the reuse of puppets across shows, the emotional significance of certain characters, and the challenges of repairing or retiring puppets. The process involves balancing historical preservation with practical restoration, maintaining the theatre's living legacy.
# Favorite Puppets and Performer Perspectives

> Alex reflects on the difficulty of choosing a favorite puppet, noting that each has unique qualities and significance. He describes how performers develop emotional connections to puppets, the learning curve involved, and the joy of discovering the magic in even the simplest designs. The abundance and diversity of puppets contribute to the theatre's enduring charm.
# Audience Experience and All-Ages Appeal

> The conversation explores the shared experience of attending Bob Baker shows, the excitement of introducing newcomers, and the theatre's appeal to all ages. Alex and Estella discuss the magic of seeing familiar and new puppets, the sense of community, and the importance of creating a space where both children and adults can feel safe and joyful. The theatre is likened to a 'third place' similar to the public library.
# Show Development, Choreography, and Rehearsal

> Alex describes the process of developing new shows, including the creation of new puppets, choreography, and rehearsal. He explains the collaborative nature of show development, the influence of Bob Baker's legacy, and the challenges of balancing tradition with innovation. The practical aspects of mounting shows, budgeting, and refining performances are discussed, highlighting the ongoing evolution of the theatre's repertoire.
# History of Bob Baker Theatre and Puppetry in Los Angeles

> Alex provides an overview of Bob Baker's early life, his first theatre in Koreatown, and his lifelong passion for puppetry. He recounts the golden age of marionettes in LA, Bob's involvement in World War II, collaborations with influential figures, and the eventual founding of the Bob Baker Marionette Theatre in 1963. The theatre's history is intertwined with a broader community of artists and designers, reflecting its deep roots in Los Angeles.
# Moving from Echo Park to Highland Park: Challenges and Community

> Alex discusses the emotional challenges of moving the theatre from Echo Park to Highland Park, the uncertainty surrounding the old location, and the collective effort to find a new home. He describes the community's involvement, the practical and financial hurdles, and the sense of momentum and hope that accompanied the transition. The new location quickly became a welcoming space, thanks to the dedication of volunteers and supporters.
# Preserving Memories and Symbols in the Move

> The conversation focuses on the intentional preservation of symbols and memories during the move, such as the clown marquee and other iconic elements. Alex explains the collaborative process of selecting meaningful details, the emotional connections to objects, and the importance of maintaining the spirit of the theatre. The new space incorporates elements from the old location, ensuring continuity and honoring the theatre's legacy.
# Community Changes and Growth

> Alex describes the growth and changes in the Bob Baker community after the move to Highland Park. He notes the increase in audience size, the shift in staff demographics, and the theatre's role in the neighborhood. The community's commitment to keeping the theatre open is emphasized, with contributions ranging from volunteering to financial support. The theatre's expansion has brought new challenges and opportunities for engagement.

story.title: Oral History with Alex Evans from Bob Baker Marionette Theater
story.description: The interview provides a rich insight into the life and contributions of Alex Evans, the co-executive and artistic director of Bob Baker Marionette Theater. Key topics include Evans' upbringing, his move from England to America, his engagement with puppetry, and the cultural transitions involved. The discussion also covers the Bob Baker Theater's relocation and adaptation efforts, emphasizing the community-driven nature of these changes. It explores the theater's history, its pivotal role in the Los Angeles arts scene, and its future plans, including greater sustainability and community involvement. The narrative underscores the theater's legacy and innovation in puppetry within both historical and contemporary contexts.
